core_claim: Affective narratology (most fully developed by Patrick Colm Hogan in 'Affective Narratology: The Emotional Structure of Stories', 2011) claims that the very STRUCTURE of stories — what counts as an incident, an episode, a plot, a genre — is a systematic product of human emotion systems, not an independent formal scaffold onto which emotion is later layered. The central, organising claim is that narratives are generated by emotional goals: a story exists because emotion systems define a goal state (typically the achievement or restoration of normalcy/eudaimonia — a condition of comfort, attachment, and sustainable joy), and the plot is the trajectory of pursuing, losing, and regaining that emotionally-defined goal. A complementary empirical strand (Brewer & Lichtenstein's structural affect theory, 1982; Bal & colleagues' Multidimensional Narrative Tension Theory) makes a narrower but testable version of the claim: the ORDER in which events are disclosed (discourse structure), independent of content, generates distinct affective responses — suspense (outcome postponed), curiosity (outcome shown before its causes), and surprise (unexpected reversal). Across both strands the dimension that 'matters most' is not what emotions a story depicts but how its structure manufactures emotion in the receiver — anticipation, tension, relief, being moved.
descriptive_vs_experiential: This framework is squarely EXPERIENTIAL/AFFECTIVE, and is one of the few frameworks in the taxonomy survey that is explicitly about the felt experience of engagement rather than topical content. It is, however, experiential in two distinguishable ways. (1) Hogan's level is structural-affective: it claims that the abstract SHAPE of a plot (the goal-pursuit-loss-restoration arc, the prototype) is itself derived from emotion, so it sits between pure structure and pure feeling — it explains why certain structures feel the way they do. (2) The structural-affect/narrative-tension strand is purely experiential and discourse-level: suspense, curiosity, and surprise are properties of HOW the telling is sequenced, not of what is told. Neither strand is descriptive/topical — a romantic prototype and a heroic prototype could share identical surface content (a war, a city) yet differ entirely in emotional architecture. This is precisely the dimension HelpME2C's existing content-topical vocabulary does NOT capture.
key_dimensions: From Hogan: (1) Three cross-cultural UNIVERSAL narrative prototypes, each defined by the emotion goal that drives it — ROMANTIC (union of lovers; goal = romantic/attachment joy; obstacles = social prohibition, separation), HEROIC (restoration of legitimate social order / the in-group's normalcy; goal = group pride, justice, security; involves a usurpation + restoration cycle), SACRIFICIAL (restoration of communal/cosmic well-being via deprivation and atonement; goal = relief of collective suffering, often religious). (2) Cross-cultural MINOR/limited genres tied to specific emotion systems — attachment (parent/child separation and reunion), lust/sexual pursuit, revenge, criminal-justice/guilt. (3) NORMALCY / EUDAIMONIA as the emotional baseline state stories move away from and toward (comfort, belonging, sustainable joy). (4) EMOTIONAL GEOGRAPHY / spatial emotion — home vs exile, sacred vs profane space carrying affective valence. (5) Emotional memory and EMPATHIC vs CATEGORICAL emotion (feeling-with a character vs feeling-about a group). From structural-affect / narrative-tension theory: (6) SUSPENSE (outcome withheld, anxious anticipation), (7) CURIOSITY (outcome or fragment revealed first, drive to reconstruct the gap), (8) SURPRISE (unexpected reversal), and the claim that curiosity triggers and organises the other two; these are the discourse-order axes. Together these give two clean axis-families: emotion-goal/prototype axes (work-level) and tension-mechanism axes (sequencing-level).
cross_cultural_validity: Cross-cultural validity is Hogan's headline claim and his strongest evidential card: the three universal prototypes (romantic, heroic, sacrificial) are argued from a deliberately wide cross-cultural sample — Sanskrit drama and the Ramayana, Chinese and Japanese narrative, African oral epics, Arabic and Persian traditions, alongside the Western canon (Shakespeare, Tolstoy). Hogan's broader research programme ('The Mind and Its Stories', 2003) built the prototype claim from a corpus of canonical works selected from many literary traditions precisely to avoid Western parochialism, and this is its main methodological strength relative to most affect frameworks in this survey. CAVEATS: (a) the evidence is interpretive/literary-critical, not statistical — 'recurs across traditions' is demonstrated by close reading of selected exemplars, not by sampling representative corpora, so selection bias is a live concern; (b) the structural-affect strand (suspense/curiosity/surprise) was validated almost entirely on Western (mostly English-language) reader samples and has weaker cross-cultural backing. What plausibly breaks down cross-culturally: the SACRIFICIAL prototype is tightly bound to religious cosmologies and may map poorly onto secular contemporary TV; the emotional valence of normalcy (what counts as the 'comfortable' baseline — individual romantic union vs collective/familial harmony) is itself culturally variable, which Hogan acknowledges as variation in prototype EMPHASIS across traditions.
empirical_grounding: Mixed and strand-dependent. Hogan's prototype theory is grounded in (a) affective-science theory (basic-emotions research, appraisal theory, attachment theory) imported as explanatory scaffolding, and (b) literary-critical interpretation of cross-cultural exemplars — it is theory-driven and illustrative, NOT experimentally validated against enjoyment, completion, or preference data. It predicts what stories will look like, not which stories specific viewers will enjoy. The structural-affect / narrative-tension strand is much better grounded empirically: Brewer & Lichtenstein (1982) and the discourse-structure studies (e.g. the ScienceDirect 'Suspense, curiosity, and surprise' line) are EXPERIMENTAL — they manipulate event order in controlled texts and measure reader-rated tension/liking, showing order alone shifts affect and enjoyment. The recent 'Inducing narrative tension in the viewer through suspense, surprise, and curiosity' work extends this to viewers (film/video) and links tension to the psychology of interest and media-enjoyment. To validate LLM-extracted dimensions against this framework you would need: (1) a labelled corpus where the prototype (romantic/heroic/sacrificial/none) and dominant tension-mechanism are coded by trained humans on the same synopses an LLM sees; (2) inter-annotator agreement baselines (prototype coding is interpretive — expect moderate kappa); (3) a downstream link to behavioural enjoyment/completion data (which neither Hogan nor most tension studies provide) to show the dimensions actually predict the metric HelpME2C cares about.
operationalisability: Partially extractable, with sharply different reliability per dimension. EXTRACTION UNIT: prototype is a WHOLE-WORK property (a film 'is' fundamentally romantic or heroic); tension-mechanism (suspense/curiosity/surprise) is better captured at the ARC or even SCENE level and is genuinely hard to read off a synopsis. EXTRACTABLE FROM SYNOPSIS (moderate confidence): the three prototypes — a synopsis usually reveals whether the driving goal is union of lovers (romantic), restoration of rightful order/justice (heroic), or atonement/sacrifice for a collective (sacrificial), and the minor genres (revenge, parent/child separation, criminal-justice). NOT RELIABLY EXTRACTABLE FROM SYNOPSIS: the tension MECHANISMS, because suspense/curiosity/surprise are properties of DISCOURSE ORDER (how the work withholds and reveals), and a synopsis is itself a re-ordering that destroys the original sequencing — a synopsis of a curiosity-driven film (told backwards) reads as a normal chronological plot. FAILURE MODES: (1) an LLM will collapse prototype into genre (label any war film 'heroic') when the affective architecture may be sacrificial or anti-heroic; (2) it will infer surprise/twist from the word 'twist' in marketing copy rather than from structure; (3) it cannot distinguish empathic from categorical emotion without character-level detail often absent in synopses; (4) it will over-assign 'romantic' to any story containing a romance subplot, losing the distinction between romance-as-content and romantic-prototype-as-structure.
motivation_served: Primarily EUDAIMONIC appreciation and being-moved, plus a strong HEDONIC tension/relief loop. Hogan's normalcy/eudaimonia frame is explicitly about emotional meaning and the restoration of well-being — closer to 'being moved' and meaning-making than to light fun. The tension strand (suspense/curiosity/surprise) maps onto the HEDONIC enjoyment of arousal-then-relief and the psychology of interest. For GROUP viewing, this framework is unusually useful because it separates two trade-able motivations cleanly: one viewer may seek high-tension hedonic arousal (suspense-driven thrillers) while the other seeks eudaimonic being-moved (sacrificial/romantic prototypes). A two-person recommender can use prototype-match to find shared emotional goals (do both want restoration-of-order catharsis, or romantic union?) and tension-level to negotiate arousal tolerance, treating them as orthogonal dials rather than a single 'mood' axis.
receiver_dependence: Both kinds are present and the split is important. WORK-INTRINSIC (absolute) properties: the prototype (romantic/heroic/sacrificial) and the discourse-order tension MECHANISMS (whether the work withholds outcome, reveals-then-explains, or reverses) are structural facts about the artefact — two viewers watching the same film are exposed to the same suspense architecture. RECEIVER-DEPENDENT (relational) properties: the AMOUNT and VALENCE of emotion actually felt — whether the romantic goal resonates, whether the in-group whose order is restored is one the viewer identifies with, whether a twist is surprising (depends on viewer foreknowledge), and whether the eudaimonic baseline matches the viewer's own values. Crucially, categorical/empathic emotion is relational: a heroic prototype only delivers pride if the viewer accepts the in-group as 'theirs.' So the prototype label is roughly absolute (good for indexing the catalogue) but the affective PAYOFF is relational (needs a per-viewer model). For a two-person recommender this means prototype/tension can be precomputed per title, while resonance must be modelled per user-pair.
blind_spots: (1) COMEDY and light/hedonic enjoyment are weakly served — Hogan's prototypes are tragic/heroic/romantic-serious in flavour; comedic, absurdist, cosy, or low-stakes pleasure (a big slice of group viewing) has no clean home, and 'sensibility training' doesn't fill the gap. (2) The framework is PLOT-centric and undervalues non-plot affect — vibe, atmosphere, aesthetic pleasure, slow-burn ambient mood, character-hangout enjoyment (e.g. sitcoms, slice-of-life anime) where little 'happens' emotionally in prototype terms. (3) Synopsis-INVISIBLE: the tension mechanisms, its most empirically validated dimensions, are precisely the ones a synopsis cannot expose. (4) NEGATIVE/uncomfortable enjoyment (horror, tragedy-seeking, 'sad-on-purpose' viewing) is partially addressed via sacrifice but not the paradox of why people choose distressing affect. (5) SERIALITY — Hogan's prototypes are single-arc; long-running TV with no restoration of normalcy (open-ended prestige drama, ongoing anime) strains the goal-restoration model. (6) It says little about HUMOUR, eroticism, or cuteness/moe as primary draws. (7) It does not predict individual preference — it explains structure's potential to evoke emotion, not which viewer will be moved.
vocabulary_implications: Recommendation: add a small, dedicated AFFECTIVE-STRUCTURE facet rather than scattering these into the existing topical theme list, because they operate on a different axis (how-it-feels vs what-it's-about). PROPOSED SLUGS — emotion-goal/prototype family (whole-work, synopsis-extractable, moderate confidence): 'romantic-prototype' (driving goal = union of lovers against obstacle), 'heroic-restoration' (usurpation then restoration of rightful order), 'sacrificial-atonement' (suffering/sacrifice to restore a collective). Minor-genre slugs that overlap existing vocab: keep 'vengeance-and-its-cost' (Hogan's revenge), 'parent-child-rift' (his attachment/separation), 'guilt-and-atonement' (his criminal-justice/sacrifice) — these are VALIDATED by Hogan. TENSION-MECHANISM family — flag as NOT reliably extractable from synopsis and gate behind a different data source: 'suspense-driven', 'curiosity-driven' (mystery-gap, withheld causes), 'twist-driven' (surprise/reversal). For these, prefer signals beyond the synopsis — runtime/episode pacing, editorial tags, the existing 'nonlinear-storytelling' slug (which is the structural cousin of curiosity-driven), or user-tag mining. ADD an affective-INTENSITY scalar ('tension-level: low/medium/high') as a per-title dial usable in group trade-offs. REMOVE/AVOID: do not add a generic 'emotional' slug — it is uninformative; force the choice of prototype or tension-mechanism. Explicitly flag to the team: prototype labels are interpretive — expect lower LLM agreement than topical themes, and validate kappa before shipping.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: VALIDATES several existing slugs by giving them an affective-structural rationale: 'forbidden-love' / 'unrequited-longing' / 'redemption-arc' are surface instances of the ROMANTIC prototype; 'underdog-rise' / 'rise-and-fall' / 'vigilante-justice' instantiate the HEROIC restoration cycle; 'guilt-and-atonement' / 'vengeance-and-its-cost' / 'betrayal-and-forgiveness' map onto the SACRIFICIAL and revenge/criminal-justice minor genres; 'grief-and-loss' and 'parent-child-rift' map onto Hogan's attachment/separation genre. 'nonlinear-storytelling' and 'slow-burn' under Texture are the structural-affect cousins of curiosity-driven and suspense pacing respectively — the framework suggests re-reading them as affective-pacing markers, not just formal labels. MISALIGNMENT it reveals: the existing vocabulary is overwhelmingly TOPICAL/descriptive (what a story is about) and has almost no slot for HOW the telling generates feeling — suspense/curiosity/surprise have no home, and there is no notion of an emotion-GOAL or restoration arc. GAPS revealed: (1) no affective-intensity/tension dial; (2) no prototype-level grouping that would let two slugs ('forbidden-love' and 'unrequited-longing') be recognised as the same emotional architecture for matching; (3) no encoding of the eudaimonic-vs-hedonic motivation split that this framework cleanly supplies for group trade-offs.
key_sources: Patrick Colm Hogan, 'Affective Narratology: The Emotional Structure of Stories' (Univ. of Nebraska Press, 2011) — book, paywalled; Project MUSE https://muse.jhu.edu/book/12270/ (institutional), publisher https://www.nebraskapress.unl.edu/nebraska/9780803230026. Hogan, 'The Mind and Its Stories: Narrative Universals and Human Emotion' (Cambridge UP, 2003) — the cross-cultural prototype foundation; paywalled. Brewer & Lichtenstein (1982), structural affect theory — foundational, often paywalled but widely summarised. 'Suspense, curiosity, and surprise: How discourse structure influences the affective and cognitive processing of a story' (Discourse Processes / ScienceDirect) https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0304422X99000212 — paywalled abstract; PDF on ResearchGate https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222121244. 'Inducing narrative tension in the viewer through suspense, surprise, and curiosity' (Poetics/ScienceDirect 2022) https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0304422X22000262 — open PDF https://uvadoc.uva.es/bitstream/handle/10324/52500/Inducing-narrative-tension.pdf (freely accessible). [uncertain] exact framing of the Multidimensional Narrative Tension Theory of Enjoyment is drawn from secondary summaries, not the full text.
